channel.title: NYT > Most Recent Headlines
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: El controvertido médico que promete cambiar el color de tus ojos
item.description: Francis Ferrari, un oftalmólogo francés, desarrolló un polémico procedimiento para cambiar el color de los ojos. Personas de todo el mundo están dispuestas a asumir el riesgo.
item.dc:creator: Saskia Solomon and Elliott Verdier

media:content.media:credit: Elliott Verdier para The New York Times

item.title: El hombre que gritó ‘gooooooooool’
item.description: Andrés Cantor, comentarista de Telemundo, es una celebridad del fútbol que actualmente participa en su décima Copa Mundial masculina. Pero su gran habilidad física no es la finta precisa de Messi ni la velocidad de Mbappé. Es su voz.
item.dc:creator: Emmanuel Morgan and Steven Molina Contreras

media:content.media:credit: Steven Molina Contreras for The New York Times

item.title: Iran’s World Cup Elimination Ends a Politically Charged Odyssey
item.description: The Iranian team’s departure from the tournament ends one of the most politically charged odysseys in recent sports history. Its soccer federation criticized the United States and praised Mexico.
item.dc:creator: Tariq Panja
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Manu Fernandez/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Iran’s players leaving the field after their game against Egypt in Seattle on Friday.

item.title: La biblioteca de Franklin añade libros sobre sexualidad a su colección
item.description: La Library Company of Philadelphia, creada en 1731 por Benjamin Franklin, ha recibido una donación de 1500 volúmenes sobre sexualidad que se remontan al siglo XVII.
item.dc:creator: Ralph Blumenthal and Caroline Gutman

media:content.media:credit: Caroline Gutman for The New York Times

item.title: Pride March Caps Joyous New York June While Threats Mount
item.description: The annual celebration of the L.G.B.T.Q. community comes as the Trump administration has targeted several New York City hospitals that have provided care for transgender young people.
item.dc:creator: Liam Stack
item.category: Same-Sex Marriage, Civil Unions and Domestic Partnerships

media:content.media:credit: Natalie Keyssar for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Cheerleaders at the New York City Pride March in 2025. The annual parade is the marquee event in a series of Pride festivities in New York throughout the month of June.

item.title: What’s the Hottest Spot in New York for Campaigning? The Club.
item.description: Zohran Mamdani did it last year. So did Claire Valdez in her recent run for Congress. Some candidates are campaigning after dark to court young voters.
item.dc:creator: Troy Closson

item.title: Central Park Horse-Drawn Carriage Rides Resume, as Push for Ban Intensifies
item.description: The rides were temporarily shut down after Romanch Mahajan, 18, fell off a carriage and died. A City Council hearing next month will weigh a ban.
item.dc:creator: Caitlyn Freeman

media:content.media:credit: Dave Sanders for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: A fatal crash earlier this month was the most recent incident involving horse-drawn carriages in Central Park. The City Council plans to discuss a possible carriage ban in July.

item.title: ‘After About 10 Minutes, a Car Pulled Up to the Traffic Light’
item.description: Our special series of columns marking Metropolitan Diary’s 50th anniversary continues with more notable contributors sharing their New York City stories.

item.title: NYT Spelling Bee Answers for June 28, 2026
item.description: Feeling stuck on today’s puzzle? We can help.
item.dc:creator: New York Times Games

item.title: China Confirms Death of Pilot Who Flew Small Plane Into Beijing’s Tallest Tower
item.description: The statement came a day after online images showed falling debris and plane parts. Another 13 were injured in the incident five miles from Communist Party headquarters.
item.dc:creator: Lily Kuo, Pei-Lin Wu and Keith Bradsher

media:content.media:credit: Keith Bradsher/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: A hole was still visible on Sunday morning on the eastern face of Citic Tower in Beijing, after a light plane hit it on Friday.

item.title: Los nuevos ataques de Irán y EE. UU. entorpecen la recuperación del tráfico marítimo en el Golfo
item.description: Los ataques se produjeron después de que el tráfico por el estrecho de Ormuz alcanzara los niveles más altos desde el inicio de la guerra de Estados Unidos en Irán.
item.dc:creator: Peter Eavis and Jenny Gross
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Reuters
media:content.media:description: Irán ha intentado establecer un control formal sobre el tráfico marítimo en el estrecho de Ormuz, algo que antes no tenía.

item.title: The Real A.I. Race Isn’t America vs. China
item.description: Washington is starting to follow a similar playbook to Beijing when it comes to artificial intelligence policy.
item.dc:creator: Dan Wang and Julian Gewirtz

item.title: A Mayor Is Taking Maternity Leave in Japan. Some Men Are Furious.
item.description: Shoko Kawata is the first mayor to take maternity leave in Japan’s history. Her decision has prompted debate about gender discrimination in the work force.
item.dc:creator: Javier C. Hernández and Kiuko Notoya

media:content.media:credit: Noriko Hayashi for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Shoko Kawata, the mayor of Yawata, in her office this month. She will be the first Japanese mayor to take maternity leave.

item.title: No Corrections: June 28, 2026
item.description: No corrections appeared in print on Sunday, June 18, 2026.

item.title: Drones Used to Fight Mosquitoes as Dengue Fever Surges in Sri Lanka
item.description: The military has joined the battle against the insects, as an outbreak strains hospitals in a country already reeling from energy shortages caused by the Iran War.
item.dc:creator: Pamodi Waravita

media:content.media:credit: Ishara S. Kodikara/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
media:content.media:description: A municipal worker fumigating on Wednesday during a nationwide three-day drive to eliminate breeding sites for the dengue-carrying Aedes mosquito in Colombo, Sri Lanka.

item.title: Quote of the Day: New York’s Dive Bars Have a Rich Past. Do They Have a Future?
item.description: Quotation of the Day for Sunday, June 28, 2026.

item.title: The City Where Colombia, and Much of Latin America, Plays as the Home Team
item.description: Miami has hosted some of the biggest events in South American and Caribbean sports, including Colombia’s match on Saturday. Brazil and Uruguay have also played World Cup games there.
item.dc:creator: Patricia Mazzei

media:content.media:credit: Scott McIntyre for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Colombian fans appeared to vastly outnumber Portugal fans when the two countries played each other in Miami on Saturday.

item.title: Metropolitan Diary Challenge Day 1: What Is Your N.Y. Story?
item.description: Over the next two days, we’ll help you craft your own Metropolitan Diary entry. Today, you’ll start by finding the moment you want to write about.
item.dc:creator: Ed Shanahan and Agnes Lee

item.title: Julia Letlow, Lifted by Trump, Wins Republican Senate Runoff in Louisiana
item.description: If she reaches the Senate, Ms. Letlow would replace Senator Bill Cassidy and become the first G.O.P. woman to represent Louisiana in the chamber.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk and Emily Davies

media:content.media:credit: Kathleen Flynn/Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Representative Julia Letlow, who is running for Senate in Louisiana with the support of President Trump.

item.title: Pro-Transgender Candidate Is Chased From a Trans Rights Event Over Gaza
item.description: Scott Wiener, who is running to replace Nancy Pelosi, was surrounded by a small group of protesters shouting invective before a transgender march in San Francisco.
item.dc:creator: Katie Benner and Kellen Browning

media:content.media:credit: Cayce Clifford for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Scott Wiener in San Francisco last year.

item.title: How Ordinary Venezuelans Are Leading the Search for Survivors
item.description: Days after two deadly earthquakes struck Venezuela, people are digging through the rubble to find their loved ones. The New York Times spoke with friends and relatives of two university students who were missing.
item.dc:creator: Edwin Corona Ramos, Ang Li, Jackeline Luna, Daniel Fetherston and Meg Felling

media:content.media:credit: Edwin Corona Ramos for The New York Times

item.title: Deadly Floods Hit Kentucky
item.description: Gov. Andy Beshear of Kentucky declared a state of emergency after deadly floods swept through parts of the state.
item.dc:creator: Alisa Shodiyev Kaff and Julie Yoon

media:content.media:credit: Greg Eans/The Messenger-Inquirer, via Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Drivers maneuvered their vehicles through flooded roads after a heavy rainfall in Owensboro, Ky., on Saturday.

item.title: U.S. Officials Said to Be Frustrated With Machado’s Call for Help
item.description: María Corina Machado, the exiled leader of the Venezuelan opposition, hopes to go home. U.S. officials say her wishes to do so come at an inopportune time.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager

media:content.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Civilians searching for survivors in a collapsed residential building in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Thursday.

item.title: Rescue Workers and Aid Arrive in Venezuela, Official Says
item.description: More than 1,600 rescue workers have arrived and 25 flights filled with aid will land over the next day in Venezuela after earthquakes devastated the country on Wednesday, according to Venezuela’s vice minister of foreign affairs, Oliver Blanco.
item.dc:creator: Cynthia Silva

media:content.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Civilians looking for survivors on Thursday in the wreckage of a residential building in La Guaira, Venezuela.

item.title: A Rush of Volunteers in Venezuela Has Slowed Rescue Efforts
item.description: The outpouring of volunteer aid after Venezuela’s earthquakes clogged the only road into the disaster zone, delaying rescue crews.
item.dc:creator: Genevieve Glatsky, María Victoria Fermín and Fabiola Ferrero

media:content.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The Pérez de León Hospital medical team in a truck in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Friday night. The doctors were handing out food, water and medical supplies to people on the streets.

item.title: The Artist Uman’s Technicolor Paintings of Rural Life
item.description: Ahead of a survey exhibition, Uman discusses skipping art-world functions, living on a farm and making work on the floor.
item.dc:creator: Lauren O’Neill-Butler

item.title: Michigan Couple Are Charged With Murder After Death of Morbidly Obese Son
item.description: Seven-year-old Casper O’Brien weighed 255 pounds when he died last year. Prosecutors said he was bedridden and subsisted on little more than snack foods.
item.dc:creator: John S.W. MacDonald

media:content.media:credit: Paul Sancya/Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Genesee County prosecutors in Michigan charged Damien and Jessica O’Brien with torture, second-degree murder and three counts of second-degree child abuse in connection with the death of their 7-year-old son.

item.title: Down in the Valley
item.description: Rebecca Goldstein and Christina Iverson’s puzzle posits a sophisticated thesis on the American vernacular.
item.dc:creator: Caitlin Lovinger

media:content.media:credit: Gregory Boissy/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
media:content.media:description: In which a couple of crossword constructors get totally tubular.

item.title: Trump Picks Lance Schroyer as New ICE Director
item.description: The president said he was nominating Lance Schroyer, an adviser to Markwayne Mullin, the D.H.S. secretary, to lead the high-profile agency.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager

media:content.media:credit: Oklahoma Highway Patrol via Reuters
media:content.media:description: Lance Schroyer, pictured in 2016. He recently joined ICE and appears to have limited national experience.

item.title: ¿Quién hizo la estatua gigante de Messi en Argentina?
item.description: Mientras Lionel Messi persigue otro título mundial, un artista se apresura a terminar una gigantesca estatua del astro del fútbol en medio del desierto patagónico.
item.dc:creator: Emma Bubola and Sarah Pabst

media:content.media:credit: Sarah Pabst for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The giant statue of Lionel Messi made by Aldo Beroisa in the remote oil town of Cutral Có, Argentina.

item.title: 12 Hours With Venezuelan Doctors Searching for Earthquake Survivors
item.description: A medical team set out from the capital to rescue people in the hardest-hit part of the disaster zone, La Guaira. They found silence in the ruins instead.
item.dc:creator: Fabiola Ferrero and Julie Turkewitz

media:content.media:credit: Fabiola Ferrero for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Dr. Zaira Medina, left, hugging a rescue worker outside her collapsed apartment building in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Friday.

item.title: Texas Has a New Way to Teach History: In Chronological Order
item.description: Most states teach history by subject. Texas will go chronologically, starting with ancient history and reaching World War II by seventh grade.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Mervosh

media:content.media:credit: Joel Angel Juarez/Reuters
media:content.media:description: The Texas State Board of Education approved new social studies standards, which will rewrite how millions of elementary and middle school students learn about history.

item.title: At Least 4 Dead in Kentucky Flooding, Governor Says
item.description: Gov. Andy Beshear of Kentucky said emergency crews were rescuing people from flooded homes and vehicles on Saturday. Rain was expected to continue into the night.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker and Sarah Mervosh

media:content.media:credit: Greg Eans/The Messenger-Inquirer, via Associated Press
media:content.media:description: Drivers maneuvered their vehicles through flooded roads after a heavy rainfall in Owensboro, Ky., on Saturday.

item.title: A Close Call With an Alberta Grizzly Bear, Recorded in Its Entirety
item.description: A woman in Alberta, Canada, recorded the bear as it circled her and her dog near a campsite and lodge.
item.dc:creator: Emmett Lindner

media:content.media:credit: Artur Widak/NurPhoto, via Getty Images

item.title: Grizzly Bear Gets Too Close for Comfort During Dog Walk
item.description: A woman in Alberta, Canada, who could not be reached for comment, had an unexpected run-in during her morning dog walk, with a persistent follower.
item.dc:creator: Zach Wasser

item.title: White House Releases Images of the Trump ‘Patriot Passport’
item.description: The State Department said it planned to release 40,000 U.S. passports featuring an image of the president to commemorate the United States’ 250th anniversary.
item.dc:creator: Mark Walker

media:content.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: A second version of a limited-release passport has a page dominated by a monochrome portrait of Mr. Trump leaning forward against the backdrop of the Declaration of Independence.

item.title: In the Ruins of Venezuela’s Earthquake, Civilians Volunteers Fill the Gaps
item.description: Volunteers have rushed to help earthquake victims, loading vehicles with medical supplies, shovels and other tools to assist rescue and recovery efforts.
item.dc:creator: Genevieve Glatsky

media:content.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Civilians and trained emergency workers looked for survivors in La Guaira, Venezuela, on Thursday. La Guaira was the state hit hardest by the earthquakes.

item.title: La economía de Venezuela parecía renacer. Entonces golpearon los terremotos
item.description: El gobierno venezolano, falto de liquidez y que ya luchaba por controlar la tasa de inflación más alta del mundo, ahora debe encontrar la manera de organizar una enorme respuesta ante el desastre.
item.dc:creator: Simon Romero and Emma Bubola

media:content.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez para The New York Times
media:content.media:description: Un edificio residencial que se derrumbó durante los terremotos consecutivos del jueves en La Guaira, Venezuela.

item.title: F.A.A. Investigates Drone Encounter at Newark Airport
item.description: The crew of a Boeing 737 arriving from Florida on Friday reported a drone in the airspace as the plane approached Newark Airport. The plane landed safely.
item.dc:creator: Maia Coleman

media:content.media:credit: Dakota Santiago for The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The F.A.A. said it receives more than 100 reports of drone sightings near airports every month.

item.title: Now It’s Fireworks and a Fence Keeping Tourists From the Reflecting Pool
item.description: Chain-link fencing now surrounds the pool in preparation for a Fourth of July celebration. It has not stopped people from gawking.
item.dc:creator: Clarence Williams

media:content.media:credit: Andrew Leyden/Getty Images
media:content.media:description: A new fence surrounds the Lincoln Memorial Reflecting Pool in Washington, D.C.

item.title: Louisiana U.S. Senate Primary Runoff 2026: Live Election Results, Crockett vs. Davis
item.description: Get live results and maps from the 2026 Louisiana runoff election.

media:content.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Louisiana Primary Runoff 2026: Live Election Results
item.description: Get live results and maps from the 2026 Louisiana primary runoff elections.

media:content.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Tony Brown, Host of Public Affairs Show Aimed at Black Audiences, Dies at 93
item.description: On “Tony Brown’s Journal,” he interviewed guests like Jesse Jackson and Lena Horne, and made programming decisions by asking himself, “Will it help Black people?”
item.dc:creator: Joseph Berger

media:content.media:credit: Afro American Newspapers/Gado via Getty Images
media:content.media:description: Tony Brown, left, in 1974, interviewing the civil rights activist William H. Murphy Sr.

item.title: N.Y.C. City Council Employee Freed After 5 Months in ICE Detention
item.description: Rafael Andres Rubio Bohorquez is back in New York City and working at City Hall after his release. Immigration officials have appealed his asylum.
item.dc:creator: Ana Ley

media:content.media:credit: Todd Heisler/The New York Times
media:content.media:description: The detention of Rafael Andres Rubio Bohorquez caused outrage among elected officials in New York City.

item.title: How the Internet Polarizes Men and Women
item.description: Is the internet polarizing the sexes? On “Interesting Times,” the author Louise Perry argues that our modern connectivity is exposing the previously private worlds of vulgar locker room talk and unkind gossip — and is driving young men and women apart.

item.title: Newly Discovered Spider Captures Ants in Spring-Loaded Trap
item.description: The ballista spider discovered in Queensland, Australia, was named after an ancient Roman crossbow, and creates a spring-loaded web to catch its prey.
item.dc:creator: Christina Kelso and Alisa Shodiyev Kaff

item.title: The July Monthly Bake: Peaches and Cream Victoria Sandwich
item.description: This simple but showstopping dessert layers light-as-air sponge cake, pillowy whipped cream and jammy peach compote.
item.dc:creator: Vaughn Vreeland

media:content.media:credit: Julia Gartland for The New York Times. Food Stylist: Rebecca Jurkevich.
media:content.media:description: Vaughn Vreeland’s peaches and cream Victoria sandwich.

item.title: Deal With Israel Divides Lebanese, Fueling Protests in Beirut
item.description: Supporters welcomed the agreement as a step toward curtailing Iran’s influence within Lebanon, but others took to the streets, calling it a capitulation.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

media:content.media:credit: Ibrahim Amro/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
media:content.media:description: People in Beirut protesting against the agreement that was signed late Friday between Israel, Lebanon and the United States.

item.title: How Do I Stop Guests From Bringing Their Children to My Parties?
item.description: A reader is frustrated by the handful of friends who assume their children are welcome at events to which they are not explicitly invited.
item.dc:creator: Philip Galanes

media:content.media:credit: Miguel Porlan

item.title: Chaotic Rush to Help Venezuela Earthquake Victims Delays Some Rescues
item.description: A surge of people rushed into the devastated earthquake zone to offer help in northern Venezuela, slowing the advance of emergency responders. The death toll linked to twin quakes earlier in the week surpassed 1,400.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times

media:content.media:credit: Adriana Loureiro Fernandez for The New York Times
